topic: wine pairing argument with the sommelier

turns.content: I almost threw a saucepan at our sommelier tonight. Not literally. Almost literally
That's a strong reaction. What did the sommelier do to earn a near-saucepan experience?

So I developed this new dish for the spring menu — pan-seared veal sweetbreads with a morel cream sauce, pickled ramp relish, and a black garlic jus. It's rich, it's earthy, it's complex. I've been working on it for weeks. And this guy — Philippe, he's been here six months — he wants to pair it with a fucking orange wine
Orange wine with sweetbreads and morel cream? That's a... bold choice on his part. What's his reasoning?

He says the tannin structure of the orange wine will "cut through the richness" and the funky oxidative notes will "complement the earthiness of the morels." And I'm standing there thinking — non, non, non. The whole point of the dish is the richness. You don't cut through it, you ride it. A good white Burgundy — a Meursault, maybe a Puligny — that's what this dish needs. Butter meets butter. The wine should amplify, not fight

You're describing two fundamentally different pairing philosophies — contrast versus complement. He wants the wine to push against the dish, you want it to harmonize. Both are legitimate approaches in theory, but the question is which one serves THIS specific dish better.

Exactement. And I know my dish. I built every layer of it. The morel cream is already doing the work of adding earthy complexity — if you throw an oxidative orange wine at it, the flavours collide instead of layering. The whole thing becomes muddy. A clean, mineral Burgundy gives the palate somewhere to rest between bites. It's like... architecture. You need open space

That's a compelling argument — if the dish itself already contains internal contrast (the richness of the cream against the acidity of the pickled ramps, the earthiness of the morels against the sweetness of the black garlic), then the wine doesn't need to add more tension. It needs to be the structural backbone. Is this something the head chef weighs in on, or is it between you and Philippe?

Chef Antoine usually lets us sort it out. He trusts me on food, trusts Philippe on wine, and expects us to find the middle. But Philippe is new and he's trying to prove he's not boring. Every pairing he proposes is some natural wine, some skin-contact thing, some amphora-aged whatever. I like natural wine! I drink it at home! But not every dish needs to be a statement about how avant-garde your wine list is

It sounds like the disagreement is partly about the specific pairing and partly about a larger pattern — he's defaulting to unconventional choices as an identity move rather than reading each dish on its own terms. Have you told him that directly?
I told him tonight that if he puts an orange wine next to my sweetbreads I will personally drink the entire bottle myself and pair the dish with water. He laughed. I was not joking. Tabarnac
An effective threat. So where did you leave things — is the Burgundy winning, or is this still an active battle?

He said he'd bring in two bottles tomorrow and we'd do a blind tasting with the dish. His orange wine against my Meursault. Fine. I'll win on merit. My dish, my rules. But I respect that he wants to taste it side by side — that's how it should work. Science, not ego. Even if I know I'm right

A blind tasting is honestly the best possible resolution — let the dish decide. And if his orange wine somehow works better than you expect, at least you'll have learned something. But I suspect your Meursault is going to do exactly what you're predicting. Let me know how it goes.
I will. And if by some miracle the orange wine wins, I will eat my words. But I won't enjoy it. Bonne nuit
Bonne nuit. May the Meursault prevail.
